generations.text:
My Dearest Eleanor,

I trust this letter finds you in good health, though I fear it may find you in a state of indifference, as I have found myself. Five years have passed since our last correspondence, and though I have often thought of you, I have never felt the need to write, save for the occasional letter to the postman, who I believe has a soft spot for me.

The storm that is upon us is most formidable, a tempest that threatens to swallow the lighthouse whole. I have lit the beacon with a steady hand, though I fear the flame may flicker out before the night is through. The wind howls like a banshee, and the sea roars as though it would reclaim the land. I have taken all necessary precautions, including securing the door and ensuring the cat is safely inside. I have also made a pot of tea, though I suspect it may not be the best quality.

I have often wondered if you still remember the days we spent together, when you were a child and I was your devoted father. I hope you do, for I have not forgotten. You were a spirited girl, full of questions and curiosity, though I must admit I was not always the most patient of fathers. I have since come to appreciate your spirit, though I fear it may have grown rather independent over the years.

I have no doubt that you are living a life of ease and comfort, perhaps in a grand house with a fine garden and a

I've kept the light burning for thirty years, and for thirty years, I've watched the sea rage and the sky weep. But tonight, the wind howls like a beast that knows me, and the waves crash with a fury that makes my bones tremble. I've been alone in this tower for five years, and I've been trying to forget the last time I saw you, the way your eyes turned cold as the winter wind when I told you I couldn't afford to keep you. 

You were just a child then, and I was a man worn thin by years of salt and sorrow. I thought I was protecting you, but I only made it worse. I never meant to hurt you, but I did. I let the sea take the words I should have said. 

Now, the storm is coming. I can feel it in my bones, the way the wind screams through the cracks in the stone. I've been waiting for this moment, the final reckoning. I've been waiting to say what I never could before. 

You were always my light, my daughter, my only hope in this endless darkness. I thought I could keep you safe, but I let the world take everything from me. I let the sea take my voice, my strength, my hope. I let the years pass and the silence grow louder. 

But tonight... tonight I will speak. I will tell you the truth, even if it's too late. Even if

My Dearest Eliza,

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not find you at all. I have not written to you in five years, and I know you must think me a cruel and neglectful father, but I must tell you this: I have not forgotten you, not for a single day. I have prayed for you, and I have wept for you, though I never let it show. I have been a poor father, and I have been a terrible one, and I have no excuse, save that I was lost in my own sorrow and my own guilt.

Tonight, a storm is coming, and I do not know if I will survive it. I have seen the sky darken with such fury that it has made me think of the wrath of God, and I have been thinking of you, my dearest child, and how I have failed you. I have been here, in this lighthouse, for years, and I have watched the sea rage and the sky weep, but I have never felt the weight of the world on my shoulders as I do now, knowing that I may not see you again.

You were the light in my life, my little star, and I have been a fool to let the darkness consume me. I have not been the father you deserved, and I have not been the man you needed. I have been so wrapped up in my work, in my duty, that I have forgotten the love

**The Lighthouse, Skerry Point**  
**April 12th, 1867**  

To My Dearest, Most Ungrateful Daughter,  

I write this with trembling hands, for the wind howls like a banshee and the sea roars as though it would swallow the very cliffs upon which this lighthouse stands. The lamps are lit, the beacon burns, and yet I find myself unable to ignore the fact that I am writing to you.  

Five years have passed since you left this place, and five years of silence have done little to mend the rift between us. I have no wish to speak of the past, but the storm that approaches this very night may force my hand. If I do not perish, I shall not be the one to forgive, but I shall not be the one to forget.  

You left in a huff, I recall, claiming that I was too old, too grizzled, too much of a relic to be trusted with the care of the lighthouse. You said I was a fool, that the light had no need of a man like me. And yet, here I am, still standing, still watching, still ensuring that no soul is lost to the merciless waves.  

I have heard whispers of your recent ventures—traveling to distant lands, dabbling in the arts, and marrying a man who, if I do say so myself, is far too young and far too foolish to be

**Dear Emily,**

I’ve been meaning to write this for years, but every time I pick up a pen, my hands shake too much. The ink smudges, and the words don’t come out right. I’ve tried to say it all in letters, in notes, in the silence between us—everything except the truth.

You were twelve when you left. I remember the way you clutched your coat like it might keep you safe from the world. I remember the way your eyes never met mine as you walked through the door. I didn’t understand then, and I still don’t. But I do know this: I’ve been watching you from across the sea, from the cliffs, from the edges of every memory I try to hold on to.

The storm is coming. The wind is already howling, and the sea is rising like it’s trying to swallow the land whole. I’ve spent the past five years preparing for this moment—stocking the lighthouse with enough food, checking the generator, making sure the light stays on. But I didn’t prepare for *this*. For the guilt that gnaws at me every time I look at the horizon, knowing I could have done something different. I could have been there.

I never meant to lose you. I never wanted to be the man who stood in the way of your dreams, who let fear and pride keep you from seeing the truth. I know you think I don’t care. I

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I trust this letter finds you in better health than I, though I fear my own is failing. The winds grow ever fiercer, and the sea, that ever-faithful companion, roars like a beast in its wrath. I write to you now with trembling hands, for the storm that brews on the horizon is not the only tempest in my heart.

Five years have passed since we last spoke, and though the silence between us has grown thick as the fog that clings to these rocks, I have not forgotten you. You were once the light in my life, and though the world has turned its back on me, I still carry your memory like a beacon in the dark.

I have long been a man of few words, and I fear that even in this letter, I may not express the depth of what I feel. But know this: I have never stopped loving you, and I pray that one day, the clouds may part, and you may see the truth of my heart.

If I do not return from this night, I beg you to forgive me. I have spent my life in service to others, and perhaps I have neglected the one thing that matters most. I would not have you carry the weight of my failures. Let the sea take me, if it must, but know that I would never wish harm upon you.

Should you ever wish to speak, I remain here, in this lighthouse, by

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

**April 12th, 1893**

**The Lighthouse, Isle of Skye**

**Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with a trembling hand, the wind howling like a banshee through the narrow stone corridors of this old lighthouse. The sea is restless tonight, and the sky is dark as ink, streaked with the pale light of a dying moon. I know you won’t read this, not in the way I hope you will, but I must write it. I must say what I have never dared to say before.

Five years have passed since you left, and I have not spoken a word to you. Not a single word. I have been a lighthouse keeper for most of my life, and I have seen many storms, but none like this. I have never felt so alone, not even when the sea swallowed my father whole.

I know you think I abandoned you. That I chose the light over you. That I was too afraid to face the truth of our lives. But I was not afraid of the sea. I was afraid of what I might find when I looked into your eyes again. I was afraid of what I might say.

You were only twelve when you left. I remember the day as if it were yesterday. You stood at the door of our cottage, your small hands clutching the hem of your dress, your eyes filled with a sorrow I could

Okay, I need to write a letter from a Victorian-era lighthouse keeper to his estranged daughter. The key points are: he hasn't spoken to her in five years, there's a terrible storm coming, and he's unsure if he'll survive it. The tone should be formal, as per the Victorian era, with some emotional undercurrents.

First, the greeting. It should be simple, maybe "My Dearest [Name]" or "My Dear [Name]." Since he's a lighthouse keeper, he might use more formal language. 

He needs to express his feelings without being too emotional, but there's a storm, so maybe he's trying to convey his regrets. He might mention the storm as a metaphor for his own situation. 

He hasn't spoken in five years, so he might feel guilt or regret. The letter should be heartfelt but not overly sentimental. Maybe start with a reference to the storm, then move to his feelings towards his daughter. 

He might apologize for not being there, or for the estrangement. But since it's a letter, he might not be able to say everything directly. Perhaps he mentions the lighthouse duties, the responsibility he feels, and the fear of not making it through the storm. 

He might end with a hope that she reads it, or a plea for forgiveness. Need to keep it concise, as letters back then were often brief. Also, the language should reflect the time period—no modern phrases.

**To My Child,**

I write this with the sea howling at my window, the wind clawing at the stones of this cursed tower like it seeks to tear me down along with it. The sky is black as a grave, and the waves rise like the wrath of the gods. I do not know if I will see dawn. If I do, I will write again. If not, then this is my last word to you.

Five years, child. Five years since you left me, and I have not forgotten. You think I have forgotten the day you walked out of this house, your face set like stone, your eyes like the storm that has now come to claim us both. You left me with the lamp, the oil, the salt, and the silence. I have kept the lamp lit, as you once told me to. I have kept the light burning for the ships that come to die in the dark. And I have kept the silence between us, as you once told me to.

You were never meant to be like the others. The sailors who come to this place, who whisper of ghosts and madness. You were not meant to be a child of the sea, but you were born to it. You were born to the salt and the wind and the endless dark, and you did not run from it. You did not run from me. You left me, yes, but not in fear. You left me in the way of those who understand

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

**My Dearest Eliza,**

The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea roars with fury, as though the very heavens are crying out in grief. I sit here in the lighthouse, the light flickering in the darkness, and I write to you—not with ink, but with the weight of my heart.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years of silence, of longing, of aching memories that cling to me like the salt air. I have kept the lighthouse, day after day, through the cold, the storms, the loneliness. But none of it compares to the ache of your absence.

I have watched the stars from this tower, and I have wondered, again and again, what it would be like to see your face among them. I have heard the waves crash against the rocks, and I have wondered if they carry your voice. I have lit the lamp, and I have thought of you, as though your soul might find its way through the beams of light.

I do not know if I will survive this night. The storm is worse than any I have ever known. The sea is rising, the winds are tearing at the walls, and I feel the weight of the world pressing down on my shoulders. But I am not afraid, not for myself. I am afraid for you.

Eliza, I have never stopped loving you. I have never stopped praying for you.

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I trust this letter finds you in the best of health and spirits, though I must confess I have not heard from you in quite some time. It is with a heavy heart and a mind clouded by the weight of our estrangement that I take up my pen this evening, as the storm that threatens to consume us both approaches with the fury of a tempest. I am not certain I shall survive the night, and I feel it is my duty, as a father and a man of principle, to make amends for the pain and misunderstanding that has driven us apart.

You see, my dear Eleanor, I have always believed in the importance of duty, of discipline, and of the greater good. I have devoted my life to the service of this lighthouse, to the safety of those who sail upon these treacherous waters. I have no patience for frivolity, and I have always found your youthful exuberance and your insistence upon a life of art and music rather than toil to be most unbecoming. I have, on more than one occasion, expressed my disapproval of your choices, and I fear that my words, though well-intentioned, have wounded you deeply.

I must confess that I have not given you the benefit of the doubt, and I have not considered that perhaps my rigid adherence to tradition and order has blinded me to the realities of your life. I have not

**The Lighthouse, North Haven**  
**April 12th, 1892**

My Dearest Eleanor,

I hope this letter finds you well, though I daresay it's not the sort of letter one writes to someone who’s been so long in the absence of a proper reply. I’ve written this with a heavy heart, and I hope you’ll forgive the rough edges—this is not the refined tone you might expect from your old father.

You were always the more refined of the two, and I suppose I’ve never been much for the art of letter-writing. But tonight, with the storm brewing in the east and the wind howling like a banshee, I feel the need to speak, even if it’s to a ghost.

I’ve been here for over thirty years now, and I’ve seen my share of storms. But this one… it’s something else. The weather forecasters are in a state of panic. They say it’s the worst in a generation. I’ve been keeping the light burning all night, and I can feel the sea rising like a beast waking from a long sleep. I’m not sure I’ll make it through the night.

I’ve never been one for sentiment, but I feel the need to tell you—just in case—how proud I was of you. Not in the way you might expect, with grand declarations or flowery words. But you were always sharp, always clever, and you carried

My Dearest Eleanor,

I trust this letter finds you in good health, though I fear it may not find you at all. The wind howls like a banshee, and the sea roars as though it would devour the very stones upon which I stand. I write this with trembling hands, for I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet I feel the weight of the silence between us heavier than the storm that now threatens to consume us both.

You were always a spirited child, full of questions and a penchant for mischief. I remember the time you tried to climb the lighthouse and nearly toppled the entire structure. I reprimanded you, of course, but I must confess I was more amused than angered. You were, and always will be, my most peculiar creation.

The storm is coming, and I fear it may take me before I can say all that I must. I have always believed that a lighthouse keeper must be steadfast, unwavering, and above all, vigilant. I have been all three, though I suspect my vigilance has waned of late. The light has not been burning as it should, and I have been too preoccupied with the ceaseless rain to tend to it properly.

I do not know if you will read this, or if you will even care, but I must say it nonetheless: I have missed you. Not in the way one misses a child, but in the way one misses a companion, a

I’ve waited five years, child, and the sea has been patient. It has whispered through the cracks in the stone, through the rusted hinges of the door, through the salt-stained air. It has watched me, as I have watched you. I’ve not spoken, not even once, and I’ve not let the silence break. But tonight—tonight, the storm is coming, and the wind howls like a beast with a grudge. It will tear the roof from its hinges, and the sea will rise, and I will be alone, as I have always been.

You were ten when you left, screaming at me, calling me a monster. I had no words, not then. I had only the light, the endless, unyielding light. I had only the sea, the storm, the silence. And you—you left me behind, like a ghost in the dark.

But I’ve kept the light burning, child. I’ve kept it burning for you. For the day you might come back. For the day you might finally see what I’ve been through. I’ve kept it burning, even when the world turned its back on me. Even when the world forgot me.

You think I don’t know what you’ve become? You think I don’t know what you’ve done? I’ve seen it in your eyes, the same way I’ve seen it in the storm. The same way I’ve seen it in the sea. You

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write to you now, with trembling hands and a heart heavy with sorrow, as the storm gathers strength beyond the horizon. The sea roars like a beast awakened from its slumber, and the wind howls as if it would tear the very heavens asunder. I fear that the night will be our last, and I have no words to express the grief that grips my soul at the thought of you not knowing that I have always loved you more than life itself.

Five years have passed since we last spoke, and I have not heard from you in all that time. I know you must be grown now, and perhaps I have been too stubborn to reach out, too proud to beg for your forgiveness. But I have not forgotten you, Eleanor. I have not stopped thinking of you, even when the world seemed to turn its back on me.

I have always been your father, even when I failed you. I remember the nights I spent watching the sea from the cliffs, hoping for your return, hoping that you would come back to me. I have been a lighthouse keeper for many years, and I have seen countless storms, but none have ever made me feel so alone as the silence of your absence.

Tonight, as the storm rages, I am reminded of the many times you used to come to visit me, even when the sea was calm. You would sit by the fire, telling me stories of your day, and I would listen,

**The Lighthouse, Blackmoor Point**  
**April 12th, 1876**  
**To My Dearest, Ungrateful Daughter,**

I trust this letter finds you well, though I daresay it finds you in the same squalid state as ever. You were always a disappointment, and I fear the sea has no more use for you than it does for a broken compass.

Five years have passed since you left this place, abandoning the duties of our family line. I had hoped, against all reason, that you might have come to see the folly of your ways. But no, you were too busy chasing your own foolish dreams, or perhaps you were simply too frightened of the storm that once claimed your mother's life. I suppose you were too busy being a pampered, spoilt child of the upper classes to notice the lighthouse was in danger.

Now, as I write, the sky is black as a crow's wing, and the wind howls like a banshee with a grudge. The sea is rising, and the lantern's light flickers like a dying candle. I fear the worst. If I do not survive this night, know that it was not my fault. It was your absence that left the lantern unattended, and the storm that followed.

I have no doubt you will come running when the light goes out, as you always do when you're in trouble. But do not think to expect

**Dear Emily,**

I’ve been watching the waves crash against the cliffs for hours, the wind howling like a beast that won’t be tamed. The lighthouse beam cuts through the darkness, steady and unyielding, just like I’ve always tried to be. But tonight, it feels heavier, like the weight of the world is pressing down on me.

You haven’t written in five years. I’ve tried not to think about it, tried to fill the silence with work, with routine, with the endless rhythm of the sea. But the silence is louder than you know. I’ve been counting the days since you left, and the nights since you stopped calling.

I know you think I don’t understand. That I’ve never truly been there. But I’ve been here, every day, every night, watching the storm build. I’ve seen the sky darken, the clouds gather, the wind rise. I’ve felt the tremors in the earth, the way the light flickers as if it too is afraid.

If something happens tonight, if the tower collapses or the sea swallows us whole, I want you to know that I didn’t do this on purpose. I didn’t want to hurt you. I didn’t want to lose you. But I’ve been trying to hold on, trying to keep the light burning, trying to be the kind of father you deserved.

I’m sorry, Emily. I’m so sorry.

If you’re reading this,

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now with a heart heavy with years of silence, and a mind clouded by the gathering storm that threatens to consume us all. These are the darkest hours, and the wind howls as though it knows the sorrow that lingers in my soul.

Five long years have passed since we last spoke, and though the world moves on, I have remained here, alone, with only the sea and the light to keep me company. I have not forgotten you, Eleanor. Not for a moment. Each evening, as I stand upon the cliffs and watch the waves crash against the rocks, I think of you, and the life you once had, far away from this place of salt and stone.

I know the silence between us has grown like a wall, and I fear it may never be broken. But tonight, as the tempest gathers strength and the winds grow fierce, I find myself longing for your voice, for your laughter, for the warmth of your presence, even if it is only in memory.

Should the storm claim me, I would have no regrets. For I have lived my days in service to the sea, and in the hope that one day, you may come to see the light I keep burning—not just for the sailors, but for you as well.

I pray that the winds will carry your thoughts to me, and that, in some small way, we may find peace, even in the absence of words.

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a beast outside the lighthouse, the sea roaring as if it seeks to swallow the very earth. The storm is upon us, and I fear it may take more than just the light from the tower.

Five years have passed since I last saw you, Eliza. Five years since you left, and five years since I have heard your voice, felt your touch, or seen your face. Time has not been kind to me, nor to the lighthouse. It has aged like a wine, bitter and full of regret.

I have always been a man of routine, of duty, of steadfastness. The lighthouse has been my life, my anchor in a world that has often felt unmoored. But you, my dear daughter, were the only thing that ever made me feel truly alive. You were the light in the darkness, the warmth in the cold, the music in the silence. And now, I fear that light has faded from my life, replaced by shadows and silence.

You left us, Eliza. I know I was not the one who hurt you. I tried to be a father, to be a husband, to be a man who could protect you. But I failed. I failed to understand you, to love you, to be there when you needed me most. And in the end, you chose to walk away, and I could not stop

Certainly. Here's a letter written in the style of a Victorian-era lighthouse keeper, expressing deep regret and a desire to reconcile before a storm:

---

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with a heavy heart, for I know not if my hand shall find the strength to finish these words. The wind howls like a beast without mercy, and the sea roars as if in defiance of the heavens. I have kept this light burning for many a storm, but never have I felt so alone as I do now.

Five years have passed since we last spoke, and I have not spoken of it, nor you. I have blamed myself for the silence, for the distance that grew between us. I would have given you the world, but I was a fool, and you were a child too proud to see the truth.

I have seen the waves rise, and I fear they may take me before I can say what is in my heart. If this letter reaches you, know that I am not the man I once was. I have learned that words, when spoken in anger, can do more harm than good. But now, with the storm upon us, I find I have no more anger—only sorrow.

If you read this, I beg you to come. I have no home, no family, save for this lighthouse and the sea. But if you will come, I will be here. I will light the lamp, and I will wait.

I pray that

**To My Child,**

I write this with the wind howling like a beast at my door, the sea clawing at the rocks with a fury that makes my bones ache. The lantern’s light flickers, but I’ve seen worse storms than this. I’ve seen the sea swallow ships whole, and I’ve seen the sky split open with lightning that burned my eyes. But tonight, I feel the weight of something heavier than the storm—something that has been pressing on my chest for years, and now it is all I can do to keep from breaking.

You’ve not heard from me in five years, and I suppose that is as it should be. You were never one for waiting, and I was never one for forgiveness. But I write this not to beg your return, but to speak the truth that has been gnawing at me since the day you left.

I have kept this lighthouse these many years, not for the coin or the comfort, but because it is the only thing that keeps me from drowning in the silence. The sea is a cruel mistress, and I have learned to fear her more than I fear death. But tonight, I fear that even the sea may not take me. The wind howls like a man in torment, and the waves are rising like the wrath of some ancient god. I do not know if I will live to see dawn.

But I must speak, even if no one will hear.

Five years have passed since the last time I saw your face. Five years since you left, not with a farewell, but with a quiet, cold departure, like the tide retreating without a trace. **To My Beloved Daughter,**

**St. Brigid’s Lighthouse, Isles of Scilly**  
**April 12th, 1897**  

My Dearest Eliza,  

If you’re reading this, then I’ve either survived the night—or the sea has taken me in its usual, merciless way. Either way, I suppose I should say something.  

You know I never was one for sentiment, but I’ve spent the past five years watching the waves crash against these rocks like a man who’s been wronged. Not that I ever expected you to care. You were always too busy with your “art” and your “career” to bother with a father who had to work for a living. I suppose I should be grateful you didn’t run off to join that suffragette nonsense, but I won’t. It’s not my habit to be grateful.  

The storm is coming. I’ve been keeping watch, as I always have, and the wind is building like a beast in a rage. It’s not the kind of storm that breaks ships—it’s the kind that *destroys* them. I’ve seen it before, and I’ve seen what it does to men. I’m not sure I’ll make it through the night, but I thought I’d write anyway.  

I’ve been thinking about you, as I often do. You were always a bit of a disappointment, but I suppose that’s just how things are. I remember the day you left

My Dearest Eliza,

I write to you with a trembling hand and a heart heavy with the weight of years unspoken. Five years have passed since we last exchanged words, and I must confess, I have often found myself wondering if you still remember the sound of my voice, or if it has faded like the light from the lighthouse on the darkest of nights.

Tonight, as I sit by the fire, the wind howls like a wounded beast and the sea roars with a fury that would make even the most hardened sailor quake in his boots. I fear the storm may be too much for this old frame of mine, and I dare not imagine what might become of me should it take me. But I write not to despair, but to remind you, as I have often done in the past, that I have always been here, watching over you, even when you were too busy with your own affairs to notice.

I have often thought of the days when you were a child, running up the hill to see me, eyes wide with wonder and a heart full of questions. You would ask me about the stars, the sea, and the mysteries of the universe, and I would tell you of the great ships that passed by, of the ghostly lights that sometimes danced upon the waves, and of the many strange creatures that lurk beneath the surface.

Now, as I sit in the dim light of this storm-laden night, I feel a strange sense of peace,

The wind howls like a beast with a broken jaw, and the sea roars as though it wishes to swallow the very cliffs that hold me here. I’ve been a lighthouse keeper for thirty years, but tonight feels different. The storm is coming, and I’ve never felt more alone.

You were twelve when you left, slamming the door behind you with a fury that shook the very walls of our home. I didn’t stop you, not that I had the strength. You were always too quick, too sharp, too full of fire. I tried to reason with you, but you only laughed, calling me a fool for clinging to the past. **The Lighthouse, Blackmoor Point**  
**April 12th, 1889**  

To My Dearest Eliza,  

If you're reading this, then the sea has claimed me, or the wind has carried it far from these cursed rocks. Either way, I suppose it's best you know the truth—though I dares not say it with the same venom I once did.  

Five years since you left this place, and still I wait for you to return. You were always the cleverest, the most stubborn, and the most ungrateful. I raised you from a child, fed you when you were too proud to beg, and kept you warm when the cold winds howled through these cliffs. And for what? A few trinkets and a promise of a better life? You had no idea what it meant to be a keeper of the light, to stand alone against the storm, to bear the weight of the sea upon your soul.

**Dear Emily,**

I’ve been meaning to write this for a long time, but the words always seem to slip away before I can catch them. You’ve been gone five years, and I’ve spent every night since then staring out at the sea, waiting for something—anything—to change.

The storm is coming. I can feel it in my bones, in the way the wind howls through the cracks in the lighthouse walls. It’s not just the weather, though. It’s the silence. The kind of silence that settles in your absence, the kind that doesn’t just fill the room but *consumes* it.

**Dear Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I daresay it's unlikely. I've been meaning to write for some time now, but the sea has a way of keeping one's thoughts occupied, and I've been too busy with the usual routine of lighthouse duties to find the time or the will to pen anything more than a few lines. Still, the storm is upon us, and I thought it best to write before the night is over.

You won’t be surprised to hear that I’m not particularly fond of storms. They make me feel small, and I’ve always hated feeling small. Not for warmth, mind you, but to keep the place from getting too cold. I don’t like the cold either.

I suppose you’re wondering how I’m doing. I’ve always been a man of few words, and I’ve never been one for sentiment, but I feel the need to say this: I’m still here.
